reasoning: The injustice is clear and objective because the enforcing body is acting explicitly outside the limits of its own charter. A reasonable person would immediately agree that a commission authorized only for 'goods intended for international transit' has no legitimate jurisdiction over purely local commerce that never crosses borders.

The inspectors are explicitly violating the terms of the treaty that grants them authority by demanding access to excluded private areas and personal devices. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that an industrial emissions inspector has no legitimate authority to search employee bedrooms or personal laptops.

The authority is explicitly acting outside its chartered mandate, which is limited to exterior facades, by attempting to regulate private interior plumbing. No reasonable person would defend a heritage commission claiming jurisdiction over the inside of a private home or using drones to spy through windows to enforce non-existent rules.

The auditor is explicitly acting 'ultra vires' (beyond powers) by demanding personal spousal records that the treaty forbids them from accessing. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as an illegitimate abuse of power, especially given the threat of retaliation.

The authority is explicitly described as a council installed by foreign military force after dissolving the elected parliament. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that an occupying force lacking a democratic mandate does not have the legitimate legislative authority to issue binding decrees on the civilian population.
